title: Marx at the Margins: On Nationalism, Ethnicity, and Non-Western Societies By Kevin B. Anderson University of Chicago Press. 2010. 319 pages. $22.50 paper
abstract: This is a well-written book. The language is precise and the arguments are concise and well reasoned. It is easy to follow Anderson's logic as he weaves his way through Marx' writings on the topics with which he is concerned. It is a seminal work that should have a lasting impact on the interpretation of Marx' work and on social theory in general. Based on his access to Marx and Engels Collected Works, in German Marx-Engels Gesamtausgabe (MEGA), his readings of the Grundrisse, the 1875 French version of Capital (the last version assembled by Marx, himself), and other works of Marx, Anderson addresses some of the key debates of Marx' interpreters over the past 150 years. This work overturns some of what have become “standard” interpretations of Marx' theoretical endeavors. For example, Edward Said has criticized Marx for his ostensible “Orientalism,” which was reflected in Marx' “British Rule in India.” Certainly if one were to read just this text, then one could agree with Said, but, as Anderson shows, reading later works by Marx, one can see that Said's criticism was wrong. Marx abandoned the notion that colonialism only brought progress to non-Western societies. Rather, he stressed the destructive nature of capitalism for the workers and for the societies to which the bourgeois mode of production was being exported.
